title: Algebra 2
blurb: Unit-by-unit practice for the Algebra 2 course — transformations, complex numbers, polynomials, logs, rationals, and sequences.

units.title: Functions & Transformations
units.blurb: Function notation, domain and range, the four graph moves, absolute value, and piecewise functions.

units.title: Quadratics & Complex Numbers
units.blurb: Vertex form and completing the square, the number i, complex roots, and quadratic inequalities.

units.title: Systems & Nonlinear Models
units.blurb: 2×2 systems at speed, three variables, curves meeting lines, and feasible regions with the corner principle.

units.title: Polynomial Functions
units.blurb: End behavior, division and the remainder/factor theorems, zeros with multiplicity, and cubic+ equations.

units.title: Radicals & Rational Exponents
units.blurb: Fractional powers, radical arithmetic, equations with phantom solutions, and inverse functions.

units.title: Exponentials & Logarithms
units.blurb: Growth and decay, logs as the reverse gear, the three log laws, and solving both equation families.

units.title: Rational Functions
units.blurb: Variation, asymptotes and holes, rational-expression arithmetic, and equations with work problems.

units.title: Sequences & Series
units.blurb: Arithmetic ladders, geometric rockets, infinite sums, sigma notation, and recursion — the capstone.

forms.title: Composing functions
forms.skill: f(g(x)): evaluate the inner function first.

variants.statement: If $f(x) = 2x - 3$ and $g(x) = x^2$, find $f(g(3))$.
variants.explanation: Inside first: $g(3) = 9$. Then $f(9) = 2 \cdot 9 - 3 = 15$. Composing in the wrong order gives $(2\cdot3 - 3)^2$ — order matters.

forms.title: Vertex from vertex form
forms.skill: y = (x - h)^2 + k has vertex (h; k) — mind the sign on h.

variants.statement: The graph of $y = (x - 2)^2 + 5$ has its vertex at which point?
variants.explanation: Vertex form $y = (x - h)^2 + k$ puts the vertex at $(h; k)$ — here $(2; 5)$. The sign trap: reading $(x - 2)$ as $x = -2$ gives $(-2; 5)$.

variants.statement: The graph of $y = (x + 3)^2 + 4$ has its vertex at which point?
variants.explanation: Vertex form $y = (x - h)^2 + k$ puts the vertex at $(h; k)$ — here $(-3; 4)$. The sign trap: reading $(x + 3)$ as $x = 3$ gives $(3; 4)$.

variants.statement: The graph of $y = (x - 1)^2 - 6$ has its vertex at which point?
variants.explanation: Vertex form $y = (x - h)^2 + k$ puts the vertex at $(h; k)$ — here $(1; -6)$. The sign trap: reading $(x - 1)$ as $x = -1$ gives $(-1; -6)$.

forms.title: Evaluating a linear inverse
forms.skill: f^{-1}(c) undoes f: subtract b first, then divide by a.

variants.explanation: Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(11) = \frac{11 - (3)}{2} = 4$. Plugging $11$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $25$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(13) = \frac{13 - (-2)}{3} = 5$. Plugging $13$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $37$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(-11) = \frac{-11 - (1)}{4} = -3$. Plugging $-11$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $-43$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(12) = \frac{12 - (2)}{5} = 2$. Plugging $12$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $62$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(7) = \frac{7 - (-5)}{2} = 6$. Plugging $7$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $9$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(-5) = \frac{-5 - (3)}{-2} = 4$. Plugging $-5$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $13$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(1) = \frac{1 - (7)}{2} = -3$. Plugging $1$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $9$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(19) = \frac{19 - (1)}{3} = 6$. Plugging $19$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $58$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(5) = \frac{5 - (-3)}{4} = 2$. Plugging $5$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $17$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(-2) = \frac{-2 - (3)}{5} = -1$. Plugging $-2$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $-7$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(15) = \frac{15 - (-1)}{2} = 8$. Plugging $15$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $29$.
Undo $f$ in reverse: $f^{-1}(14) = \frac{14 - (5)}{3} = 3$. Plugging $14$ into $f$ itself — the classic inverse slip — gives $47$.

forms.title: Tracking a point through a transformation
forms.skill: Outside changes move y, inside changes move x (backwards), a leading minus flips y.

variants.statement: The point $(3; 7)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = f(x) + 4$?
variants.explanation: Adding $4$ outside $f$ slides the graph vertically, so only the $y$-coordinate moves: $(3; 11)$. Shifting the $x$-coordinate instead gives $(7; 7)$ — the classic mix-up.

variants.statement: The point $(-2; 5)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = f(x) - 3$?
variants.explanation: Adding $-3$ outside $f$ slides the graph vertically, so only the $y$-coordinate moves: $(-2; 2)$. Shifting the $x$-coordinate instead gives $(-5; 5)$ — the classic mix-up.

variants.statement: The point $(4; -1)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = f(x) + 6$?
variants.explanation: Adding $6$ outside $f$ slides the graph vertically, so only the $y$-coordinate moves: $(4; 5)$. Shifting the $x$-coordinate instead gives $(10; -1)$ — the classic mix-up.

variants.statement: The point $(-5; -3)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = f(x) + 2$?
variants.explanation: Adding $2$ outside $f$ slides the graph vertically, so only the $y$-coordinate moves: $(-5; -1)$. Shifting the $x$-coordinate instead gives $(-3; -3)$ — the classic mix-up.

variants.statement: The point $(2; 9)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = f(x - 3)$?
variants.explanation: Replacing $x$ by $x - 3$ shifts the graph 3 unit(s) right, so the point moves to $(5; 9)$. Sliding it the other way to $(-1; 9)$ is the classic backwards slip.

variants.statement: The point $(-4; 1)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = f(x + 2)$?
variants.explanation: Replacing $x$ by $x + 2$ shifts the graph 2 unit(s) left, so the point moves to $(-6; 1)$. Sliding it the other way to $(-2; 1)$ is the classic backwards slip.

variants.statement: The point $(5; -6)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = f(x - 4)$?
variants.explanation: Replacing $x$ by $x - 4$ shifts the graph 4 unit(s) right, so the point moves to $(9; -6)$. Sliding it the other way to $(1; -6)$ is the classic backwards slip.

variants.statement: The point $(1; 2)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = f(x + 5)$?
variants.explanation: Replacing $x$ by $x + 5$ shifts the graph 5 unit(s) left, so the point moves to $(-4; 2)$. Sliding it the other way to $(6; 2)$ is the classic backwards slip.

variants.statement: The point $(3; 4)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = -f(x)$?
variants.explanation: The minus in front of $f$ reflects the graph across the $x$-axis, flipping only the $y$-coordinate: $(3; -4)$. Reflecting across the $y$-axis instead gives $(-3; 4)$.

variants.statement: The point $(-2; 7)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = -f(x)$?
variants.explanation: The minus in front of $f$ reflects the graph across the $x$-axis, flipping only the $y$-coordinate: $(-2; -7)$. Reflecting across the $y$-axis instead gives $(2; 7)$.

variants.statement: The point $(5; -3)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = -f(x)$?

variants.statement: The point $(-6; -1)$ lies on the graph of $y = f(x)$. Which point must lie on the graph of $y = -f(x)$?

forms.title: Reading β from complex roots
forms.skill: When D < 0 the roots are −p/2 ± (√|D|/2)i — halve the root of |D|.

variants.explanation: $D = (2)^2 - 4 \cdot 5 = -16 < 0$, so $x = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{16}\,i}{2}$ and $\beta = \frac{\sqrt{16}}{2} = 2$. Forgetting to halve $\sqrt{16}$ gives $4$.

forms.title: Circle meets horizontal line
forms.skill: Substitute y = k into x^2 + y^2 = r^2 and count real solutions of x^2 = r^2 - k^2.

variants.statement: How many points do the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 25$ and the line $y = 3$ have in common?
variants.explanation: Substitute $y = 3$: $x^2 = 25 - 9 = 16$, giving $x = \pm\sqrt{16}$ — two points. Four is impossible: a line meets a circle at most twice.

variants.statement: How many points do the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 25$ and the line $y = 5$ have in common?
variants.explanation: Substitute $y = 5$: $x^2 = 25 - 25 = 0$, giving only $x = 0$ — the line is tangent, one point. Counting $\pm 0$ as two points is the classic slip.

variants.statement: How many points do the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 25$ and the line $y = 7$ have in common?
variants.explanation: Substitute $y = 7$: $x^2 = 25 - 49 = -24$, giving no real solutions — the line misses the circle entirely, zero points. Forgetting to compare $|7|$ with the radius $5$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: How many points do the graphs of $y = x^2 - 2$ and $y = x$ share?
variants.explanation: Set them equal: $x^2 - x - 2 = 0$ has discriminant $(1)^2 - 4 \cdot (-2) = 9$ — positive, so the graphs meet twice. Three is impossible: a quadratic has at most two roots.

forms.title: Symmetric systems: x² + y²
forms.skill: x² + y² = (x + y)² − 2xy — square the sum, subtract twice the product.

variants.statement: If $x + y = 5$ and $xy = 1$, find $x^2 + y^2$.
variants.explanation: Square the sum: $(x + y)^2 = x^2 + 2xy + y^2$, so $x^2 + y^2 = (5)^2 - 2 \cdot (1) = 23$. Forgetting to subtract $2xy$ leaves $25$.

variants.statement: If $x + y = 5$ and $xy = 2$, find $x^2 + y^2$.

forms.title: Parabola meets line by substitution
forms.skill: Set x^2 = ax + b, factor, and read off both roots before answering.

variants.statement: The parabola $y = x^2$ and the line $y = 3x + 10$ intersect at two points. What is the larger $x$-coordinate of an intersection point?
variants.explanation: Set $x^2 = 3x + 10$ and factor: $x^2 - 3x - 10 = (x - 5)(x + 2) = 0$, so $x = 5$ or $x = -2$; the larger is $5$. Grabbing the smaller root, $-2$, is the classic slip.

forms.title: Degree and leading coefficient of a product
forms.skill: Degrees add, leading coefficients multiply.

variants.statement: What is the degree of the product $(x^3 + 2x - 1)(x^4 - x + 5)$?
variants.explanation: Degrees add under multiplication: the leading terms multiply to $x^{3} \cdot x^{4} = x^{7}$. Multiplying the degrees to get $12$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: What is the leading coefficient of the product $(3x^4)(-2x^5)$?
variants.explanation: Coefficients multiply and exponents add: $(3)(-2) = -6$ on $x^{9}$. Adding the coefficients gives $1$, and $9$ is the degree — not the coefficient.

forms.title: End behavior of polynomials
forms.skill: Sign and parity of the leading term decide both ends of the graph.

variants.statement: Describe the end behavior of the graph of $p(x) = x^2 - 4x + 1$.
options: Rises to the right and rises to the left
Falls to the right and falls to the left
Falls to the right and rises to the left
Rises to the right and falls to the left
variants.explanation: The leading term $x^2$ wins for large $|x|$: even degree with a positive coefficient means the graph rises to the right and rises to the left. Reading end behavior off the lower-order terms — which the leading term swamps — is the classic mistake.

forms.title: Reading a factored cubic
forms.skill: Factored form hands you the roots: sum them, evaluate p(0), rank them.

variants.statement: For $p(x) = (x + 6)(x + 4)(x + 3)$, find the sum of the roots.
variants.explanation: Set each factor to zero: the roots are $-6$, $-4$ and $-3$, so their sum is $-13$. Flipping every root's sign (reading $(x - r)$ as root $-r$) gives $13$.
Substitute $x = 0$: each factor becomes minus its root, so $p(0) = (-4)(6)(2) = -48$. Taking the plain product of the roots, $48$, misses the three sign flips.

variants.statement: For $p(x) = (x - 3)(x - 7)(x + 6)$, what is the largest root?
variants.explanation: The roots are $3$, $7$ and $-6$; the largest is $7$. Careful with negatives: $-6$ is the smallest root, not the largest.

variants.statement: For $p(x) = (x + 5)(x + 2)(x - 7)$, what is the largest root?
variants.explanation: The roots are $-5$, $-2$ and $7$; the largest is $7$. Careful with negatives: $-5$ is the smallest root, not the largest.
Substitute $x = 0$: each factor becomes minus its root, so $p(0) = (-3)(5)(-1) = 15$. Taking the plain product of the roots, $-15$, misses the three sign flips.

forms.title: The remainder theorem

variants.statement: Find the remainder when $x^3 + x^2 + 2x - 3$ is divided by $(x - 1)$.
variants.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem, dividing by $(x - 1)$ leaves $p(1)$; substituting gives $1$. Evaluating at $x = -1$ instead — misreading the divisor's sign — gives $-5$.

forms.title: Zeros with multiplicity
forms.skill: Even multiplicity touches the x-axis; odd multiplicity crosses. Distinct zeros count once.

variants.statement: The graph of $p(x) = (x - 3)^2(x + 1)$ touches (without crossing) the $x$-axis at which zero?
variants.explanation: The squared factor makes $x = 3$ a double zero, so the graph touches and turns there; at $x = -1$ the single factor makes it cross. Picking the crossing zero is the classic mix-up.

variants.statement: Find the sum of the distinct zeros of $p(x) = (x - 2)^2(x - 3)$.
variants.explanation: The distinct zeros are $2$ and $3$, so the sum is $5$. Counting the double zero twice — as if multiplicity added a root — gives $7$.

variants.statement: The graph of $p(x) = (x + 2)^2(x - 5)$ touches (without crossing) the $x$-axis at which zero?

variants.statement: Simplify $5\sqrt{3} + 2\sqrt{3} - 4\sqrt{3}$.
variants.explanation: Like radicals combine through their coefficients: $(5 + 2 - 4)\sqrt{3} = 3\sqrt{3}$. Ignoring the minus sign gives $11\sqrt{3}$, and the radical itself never disappears.

forms.title: Evaluating rational exponents
forms.skill: n^(p/q) = (q-th root of n)^p; a negative exponent means reciprocal.

variants.statement: Evaluate $4^{3/2}$.
variants.explanation: Root first, then power: $4^{3/2} = (\sqrt{4})^{3} = 2^{3} = 8$. Skipping the root and computing $4^{3}$ gives $64$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $4^{5/2}$.
variants.explanation: Root first, then power: $4^{5/2} = (\sqrt{4})^{5} = 2^{5} = 32$. Skipping the root and computing $4^{5}$ gives $1024$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $4^{-1/2}$.
variants.explanation: The negative exponent flips to a reciprocal: $4^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{(\sqrt{4})^{1}} = \frac{1}{2^{1}} = \frac{1}{2}$. Dropping the minus gives $2$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $4^{-3/2}$.
variants.explanation: The negative exponent flips to a reciprocal: $4^{-3/2} = \frac{1}{(\sqrt{4})^{3}} = \frac{1}{2^{3}} = \frac{1}{8}$. Dropping the minus gives $8$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $8^{2/3}$.

variants.statement: Evaluate $8^{5/3}$.

forms.title: Solving a basic radical equation
forms.skill: sqrt(x + a) = b: square both sides first, then isolate x = b^2 - a.

variants.statement: Solve $\sqrt{x + 5} = 4$ for $x$.
variants.explanation: Square both sides: $x + 5 = 16$, so $x = 16 - (5) = 11$. Moving $5$ across without squaring first gives $-1$.

forms.title: Exponential equations via substitution
forms.skill: b^(2x) is (b^x)² — substitute t = b^x and solve the quadratic.

variants.statement: The equation $2^{2x} - 3 \cdot 2^{x} + 2 = 0$ has two roots. Find their SUM.
variants.explanation: Let $t = 2^{x}$: $t^2 - 3t + 2 = 0$ gives $t = 1$ or $t = 2$, so $2^{x} = 1$ ($x = 0$) or $2^{x} = 2$ ($x = 1$). The sum of the roots is $1$.

$\log_{2}(x - 3) > 2$ means $x - 3 > 2^{2} = 4$, i.e. $x > 7$ (the domain $x - 3 > 0$ is automatically satisfied).
$\log_{2}(x + 1) > 2$ means $x + 1 > 2^{2} = 4$, i.e. $x > 3$ (the domain $x + 1 > 0$ is automatically satisfied).
$\log_{2}(x + 2) > 2$ means $x + 2 > 2^{2} = 4$, i.e. $x > 2$ (the domain $x + 2 > 0$ is automatically satisfied).
$\log_{2}(x - 1) > 3$ means $x - 1 > 2^{3} = 8$, i.e. $x > 9$ (the domain $x - 1 > 0$ is automatically satisfied).

forms.title: Cancel, then evaluate
forms.skill: (x^2 - k^2)/(x - k) = x + k for x != k — factor before substituting.

variants.statement: Simplify $\dfrac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3}$, then evaluate the result at $x = 5$.
variants.explanation: Factor the difference of squares and cancel: $\frac{(x - 3)(x + 3)}{x - 3} = x + 3$, which at $x = 5$ equals $8$. Subtracting instead — as if it simplified to $x - 3$ — gives $2$.

forms.title: Hole or vertical asymptote?
forms.skill: A cancelled factor leaves a hole; an uncancelled denominator zero is a vertical asymptote.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = \dfrac{(x - 2)(x + 3)}{x - 2}$, what feature does the graph have at $x = 2$?
variants.explanation: The factor $(x - 2)$ cancels, so near $x = 2$ the graph is just $y = x + 3$ with one point missing — a hole. Calling it a vertical asymptote is the classic confusion: an asymptote needs a denominator zero that does NOT cancel.

variants.statement: For $f(x) = \dfrac{(x - 1)(x + 2)}{x - 3}$, what feature does the graph have at $x = 3$?
variants.explanation: At $x = 3$ the denominator vanishes while the numerator equals $10 \ne 0$, so $f$ blows up — a vertical asymptote. A hole would need $x - 3$ to cancel with the numerator, and it doesn't.

forms.title: Infinite geometric series
forms.skill: S = b_1/(1 − q), valid only when |q| < 1.

variants.statement: Find the sum of the infinite geometric series with $b_1 = 3$ and $q = \frac{1}{2}$.
variants.explanation: $|q| < 1$, so it converges: $S = \dfrac{b_1}{1 - q} = \dfrac{3}{1 - (\frac{1}{2})} = 6$.
